SECTION 2: STAFF REPORT SUMMARY
STAFF REPORT ID: PD-2024-06-19
AUTHOR: PLANNING DEPARTMENT STAFF
DATE: JUNE 19, 2024
SUBJECT: PRELIMINARY REVIEW OF VARIANCE REQUEST 24-010

This staff report summarizes the preliminary review of the application submitted by Crestview Holdings LLC. The application was received on June 1, 2024. The request type is Variance for Density. The property address is 1500 River Road. The applicant seeks to increase the allowable density on the lot beyond the current zoning restrictions. The current zoning allows for a maximum of 10 units per acre. The applicant requests a variance to allow for 15 units per acre.

The staff reviewed the parking documentation submitted with the application. The documentation provided was incomplete. The applicant failed to provide the required parking space calculations for the additional units. The staff noted insufficient parking documentation. The current zoning code requires 1.5 parking spaces per unit. The applicant proposed 1.2 parking spaces per unit. This proposal does not meet the minimum requirement.

The site plan shows the proposed building footprint. The building footprint covers 60% of the lot area. The maximum allowed coverage is 50%. The applicant requested a variance for lot coverage as well. The site plan shows a setback of 5 feet from the property line. The required setback is 10 feet. The applicant requested a variance for the setback requirement.

The environmental review indicates no impact on flood zones. The property is not located within a floodplain. The soil report indicates stable soil conditions. The geotechnical report was submitted on May 30, 2024. The report number is GEOT-2024-0530-02. The report was reviewed by the Engineering Department. The Engineering Department has no objections to the structural design. The Planning Department has objections regarding the parking and density.

The applicant was notified of the deficiencies on June 10, 2024. The applicant was given 10 days to correct the documentation. The deadline for correction was June 20, 2024. The applicant did not submit the corrected documentation. The applicant response was None. The staff recommends denial of the variance request. The denial is based on the failure to meet the minimum parking requirements and the lack of supporting documentation for the lot coverage variance.

The staff recommends that the Zoning Board of Appeals vote to deny variance 24-010. The denial will maintain the current zoning standards for the area. The current standards protect the character of the neighborhood. The standards ensure adequate infrastructure capacity. The standards ensure safe pedestrian access. The standards ensure adequate sunlight for adjacent properties.

The staff report was distributed to the Zoning Board members on June 20, 2024. The members received the report via email and the online portal. The report includes the site plan, the parking calculations, and the soil report. The report is available for public inspection. OFFICE OF THE CITY CLERK
100 MAIN STREET
MILLBROOK, STATE 00000

CITY COUNCIL MEETING MINUTES
REGULAR SESSION
DATE: JUNE 4, 2024
TIME: 6:00 P.M.
LOCATION: CITY COUNCIL CHAMBERS, MUNICIPAL BUILDING, ROOM 101

CALL TO ORDER

The regular session of the Millbrook City Council was called to order at 6:00 P.M. by Mayor Eleanor Vance.

ROLL CALL

Council Member Vance (Mayor) – Present
Council Member David Chen – Present
Council Member Sarah Miller – Present
Council Member Robert O'Malley – Present
Council Member Linda Thompson – Present
City Clerk Thomas J. Halloway – Present
City Manager Richard D. Sterling – Present
Public Works Director James P. Garris – Present
Planning Director Elena R. Rossi – Present
Zoning Board Administrator Marcus T. Webb – Present

Mayor Vance called the roll. All members were present. A quorum was declared.

INVOCATION AND PLEDGE OF ALLEGIANCE

The invocation was given by Reverend Arthur P. Hayes of the Millbrook Community Church. Following the invocation, the Council and the audience stood for the Pledge of Allegiance to the Flag of the United States of America.

APPROVAL OF AGENDA

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member Miller, to approve the agenda as printed and distributed.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

CONSENT AGENDA

The following items were placed on the Consent Agenda. These items are considered routine and non-controversial and will be enacted by a single motion. No separate discussion will be held unless a Council Member requests an item be removed for discussion.

1. Approval of Minutes from the Regular Session held on May 7, 2024.
2. Approval of Minutes from the Special Session held on May 21, 2024.
3. Approval of the Zoning Board of Appeals Meeting Minutes from May 15, 2024.
4. Approval of the Planning Commission Meeting Minutes from May 22, 2024.
5. Acceptance of the Quarterly Financial Report for the period ending April 30, 2024.
6. Approval of the Contract for Landscape Maintenance Services with Green Valley Landscapes, Inc. for the fiscal year 2024-2025.
7. Authorization to advertise for bids on the Downtown Street Resurfacing Project, Contract No. 2024-045.
8. Approval of the Annual Permit Fee Schedule adjustment for the fiscal year 2025.
9. Acceptance of the Report on the Annual Review of the Millbrook Housing Authority.
10. Approval of the Quarterly Contribution Report - Q2 for the Ethics Commission.

Council Member O'Malley requested the removal of Item 10 from the Consent Agenda for individual discussion regarding the filing procedures. Mayor Vance granted the request. Item 10 was moved to the Regular Agenda.

Council Member Thompson requested the removal of Item 7 from the Consent Agenda to discuss the bid specifications for the resurfacing project. Mayor Vance granted the request. Item 7 was moved to the Regular Agenda.

With Items 7 and 10 removed, the remaining items (1-6, 8-9) were considered.

Motion by Council Member Thompson, seconded by Council Member Chen, to approve the Consent Agenda as amended.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

ITEM 7: ADVERTISEMENT FOR BIDS – DOWNTOWN STREET RESURFACING PROJECT

Planning Director Elena R. Rossi presented the proposal for the Downtown Street Resurfacing Project. The project encompasses the resurfacing of Main Street from 1st Avenue to 5th Avenue, including the installation of new curb ramps, repainting of crosswalks, and the replacement of three sections of storm drain grates. The total estimated cost is $425,000. The funding source is the State Transportation Improvement Grant (STIG) 2023-2024, with a local match of 10%.

Staff Recommendation: The Planning Department recommends that the City Council authorize the advertisement for bids for the Downtown Street Resurfacing Project. The advertisement will be published in the Millbrook Gazette and the Millbrook Daily Chronicle for a period of two weeks. Bids will be accepted until 2:00 P.M. on July 15, 2024. The contract will be awarded to the lowest responsible bidder, subject to final engineering review and approval by the City Attorney.

Public Comment:
Council Member Miller asked if the project timeline accounts for the upcoming summer festival on Main Street.
Director Rossi responded that the project schedule has been coordinated with the Festival Committee. Construction is scheduled to begin on August 1, 2024, immediately following the conclusion of the festival.
Council Member O'Malley inquired about the material specifications for the asphalt mix.
Director Rossi stated that the specifications call for a polymer-modified asphalt mix to ensure durability and reduce noise levels, in compliance with the State Department of Transportation standards.
Council Member Thompson asked about the contingency fund allocation.
Director Rossi noted that the bid estimate includes a 5% contingency fund, which is standard for municipal infrastructure projects of this scope.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member O'Malley, to authorize the advertisement for bids for the Downtown Street Resurfacing Project, Contract No. 2024-045, with a total estimated cost not to exceed $425,000, subject to final engineering review.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

ITEM 10: APPROVAL OF THE QUARTERLY CONTRIBUTION REPORT - Q2 FOR THE ETHICS COMMISSION

City Clerk Thomas J. Halloway presented the Quarterly Contribution Report - Q2, Filing ID CF-2024-Q2. The report covers the reporting period from April 1, 2024, to June 1, 2024. The filing was submitted electronically by the committee treasurer and processed by the Ethics Commission Clerk. The review status is complete.

The report details contributions received by the Patterson Campaign Committee, Kowalski for ZBA, and the Rivera Campaign Fund. The total contributions received for the period were $12,500. The report notes that all contributions under $500 are exempt from detailed disclosure. No conflicts of interest were reported for this period.

The filing includes contributions from the Millbrook Chamber of Commerce, Henderson Construction, Local Bank & Trust, and Green Valley Farms. Specific donor details are as follows:
- Donor: Henderson Construction, Amount: $1,000.
- Donor: Local Bank & Trust, Amount: $2,500.
- Donor: Green Valley Farms, Amount: $500.

Payment methods included Check #4501 and Wire Transfer. Receipt number REC-2024-0045 was issued. The filing was processed by the Ethics Commission Clerk. A compliance check passed. The audit flag is None. The previous filing was Q1 2024. The next filing is scheduled for Q3 2024. The archive location is the Ethics Commission Server. The digital signature was verified. A paper copy was filed. The contact for the commission is ethics@millbrook.gov, phone (555) 019-2024, address 100 Main St, Millbrook. The discrepancy count is 0. The report was submitted electronically by the committee treasurer.

Council Member Miller asked if the contribution from Henderson Construction was related to any pending zoning applications.
City Clerk Halloway responded that the Ethics Commission maintains a database of all pending applications. As of the date of this filing, there are no pending zoning applications for Henderson Construction that would require a specific disclosure beyond the standard contribution report.
Council Member O'Malley asked about the source of the wire transfer.
City Clerk Halloway responded that the wire transfer originated from the account of Local Bank & Trust, as listed in the filing.
Council Member Thompson asked if the $500 contribution from Green Valley Farms was processed as a check or wire.
City Clerk Halloway confirmed that the $500 contribution from Green Valley Farms was processed via Check #4501.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member Miller, to approve the Quarterly Contribution Report - Q2, Filing ID CF-2024-Q2, and to file the report with the City Clerk's office for public record.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

REGULAR AGENDA ITEMS

ITEM 11: ZONING BOARD OF APPEALS HEARING – VARIANCE REQUEST FOR 450 RIVER ROAD

Planning Director Elena R. Rossi introduced the matter regarding a variance request submitted by the owner of 450 River Road, a property currently zoned R-2 (Single Family Residential). The applicant, Mr. James P. Henderson, is seeking a variance to allow the construction of a two-story accessory dwelling unit (ADU) and a detached garage structure that exceeds the current setback requirements by 4.5 feet. The proposed structure is to be used for short-term rental purposes, subject to the City's short-term rental ordinance.

The applicant states that the property is located in a high-demand area for short-term rentals due to its proximity to the Millbrook River Trail. The applicant argues that the variance is necessary to maximize the utility of the property and that the structure will not negatively impact the character of the neighborhood. The applicant has submitted a traffic impact study, an architectural rendering, and a letter of support from the immediate neighbors.

Staff Recommendation: The Planning Department recommends the denial of the variance request. The staff report notes that the proposed setback variance of 4.5 feet does not meet the criteria for a variance under Section 14.05 of the Millbrook Zoning Ordinance. The staff argues that the property is not unique in its physical characteristics and that the hardship is self-imposed. Furthermore, the staff notes that the proposed use as a short-term rental may create a nuisance for the surrounding residential area.

Zoning Board Administrator Marcus T. Webb stated that the Zoning Board of Appeals will hear the case on June 18, 2024. The Board will review the application, hear testimony from the applicant and the public, and render a decision. The Board may approve the variance with conditions, deny the variance, or remand the application for further review.

Public Comment:
Council Member Vance asked if the applicant has addressed the noise concerns raised in the traffic impact study.
Director Rossi responded that the applicant has proposed a sound barrier wall along the property line, which is included in the architectural rendering.
Council Member O'Malley asked if the short-term rental license has been issued.
Director Rossi stated that the short-term rental license has not been issued as the variance is a prerequisite for the license.
Council Member Thompson asked if the neighbors have signed the letter of support.
Director Rossi confirmed that the letter of support was signed by the owners of 448 and 452 River Road.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member Miller, to forward the variance request for 450 River Road to the Zoning Board of Appeals for a hearing on June 18, 2024, and to direct the Planning Department to prepare a comprehensive staff report for the hearing.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

ITEM 12: ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW SUMMARY – MILLBROOK WASTEWATER TREATMENT PLANT UPGRADE

Public Works Director James P. Garris presented the Environmental Review Summary for the Millbrook Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade Project. The project involves the installation of new filtration systems, the expansion of the sludge dewatering facility, and the upgrade of the disinfection system to comply with the new State Environmental Protection Standards. The total estimated cost is $2,850,000. The funding source is a combination of State Environmental Improvement Grants and City General Fund reserves.

The Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) was completed on May 15, 2024. The EIS concluded that the project would have a minimal impact on the local ecosystem. The primary concerns addressed in the EIS were potential air quality impacts during construction and the disposal of sludge. The EIS recommends the implementation of dust control measures and the use of a certified waste disposal facility for sludge.

Staff Recommendation: The Public Works Department recommends the approval of the Environmental Review Summary and the authorization to proceed with the design phase of the project. The design phase is expected to take six months. Construction is scheduled to begin in January 2025.

Public Comment:
Council Member Miller asked about the timeline for the sludge disposal facility certification.
Director Garris responded that the certification process is underway and is expected to be completed by August 2024.
Council Member O'Malley asked if the dust control measures have been budgeted.
Director Garris confirmed that dust control measures are included in the construction budget.
Council Member Thompson asked about the public notification process for the EIS.
Director Garris stated that the EIS was published in the Millbrook Gazette and available for public review at the City Library and the Public Works Department office for a period of 30 days. No public comments were received during the review period.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member O'Malley, to approve the Environmental Review Summary for the Millbrook Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade Project and to authorize the design phase.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

ITEM 13: PROPERTY TRANSFER RECORDS – PARCEL ID 2024-0892

City Clerk Thomas J. Halloway presented the Property Transfer Records for Parcel ID 2024-0892, located at 123 Oak Street. The property was sold by the estate of Margaret L. Thompson to the Millbrook Housing Authority. The sale price was $350,000. The deed was recorded on May 28, 2024. The transfer tax was paid in full. The property is zoned R-3 (Multi-Family Residential) and is intended for use as affordable housing units.

The transfer record includes the legal description of the property, the names of the buyer and seller, the purchase price, and the parcel ID. The document also includes the names of the attorneys involved in the transaction and the title insurance company. The transaction was processed through the County Recorder's Office.

Council Member Vance asked if the property is subject to any liens or encumbrances.
City Clerk Halloway responded that the title insurance policy issued by First Title Company confirms that the property is free of all liens and encumbrances.
Council Member O'Malley asked if the transfer tax was calculated based on the sale price.
City Clerk Halloway confirmed that the transfer tax was calculated based on the sale price of $350,000, in accordance with the City's Transfer Tax Ordinance.
Council Member Thompson asked if the property is included in the City's affordable housing inventory.
City Clerk Halloway responded that the property will be added to the City's affordable housing inventory upon the completion of the renovation phase.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member Miller, to accept the Property Transfer Records for Parcel ID 2024-0892 and to record the deed in the City's official records.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

ITEM 14: PUBLIC COMMENT SUBMISSIONS – ZONING AND PLANNING

The City Clerk opened the public comment period for zoning and planning matters. The following submissions were received:

1. Submission from John D. Smith, resident of 450 River Road, regarding the variance request for the property. Mr. Smith expressed concern about the potential increase in traffic and noise. He requested that the Zoning Board of Appeals deny the variance.
2. Submission from the Millbrook Neighborhood Association, regarding the downtown street resurfacing project. The Association expressed support for the project but requested that the construction schedule be adjusted to minimize disruption to local businesses.
3. Submission from Green Valley Farms, regarding the contribution to the Rivera Campaign Fund. The submission clarified that the contribution was a standard donation and not related to any specific zoning application.
4. Submission from Henderson Construction, regarding the contribution to the Patterson Campaign Committee. The submission stated that the contribution was made in accordance with the City's campaign finance laws and did not influence any council decisions.
5. Submission from Local Bank & Trust, regarding the contribution to the Kowalski for ZBA committee. The submission confirmed that the contribution was a voluntary donation and did not create a conflict of interest.

Council Member Miller asked if the submissions from the campaign finance donors were reviewed by the Ethics Commission.
City Clerk Halloway responded that the submissions were reviewed and are part of the public record. The Ethics Commission confirmed that no conflicts of interest were reported.
Council Member O'Malley asked if the submissions from the residents were addressed in the staff reports.
Director Rossi responded that the concerns raised by Mr. Smith and the Neighborhood Association were included in the staff reports for the respective hearings.

Public Comment Closed.

ITEM 15: INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMO – COORDINATION OF SUMMER EVENTS

City Manager Richard D. Sterling presented an Inter-Departmental Memo regarding the coordination of summer events in Millbrook. The memo outlines the responsibilities of various departments for the upcoming summer festival, the farmers market, and the outdoor concert series.

The memo details the following:
- Public Works Department: Responsible for setting up stages, lighting, and sound equipment.
- Police Department: Responsible for security, traffic control, and emergency response.
- Parks and Recreation Department: Responsible for vendor coordination, food service, and activity planning.
- Planning Department: Responsible for permit issuance and zoning compliance.
- Finance Department: Responsible for budget oversight and revenue collection.
- Communications Department: Responsible for public relations and media outreach.

The memo emphasizes the need for close coordination between departments to ensure the success of the events. A joint planning meeting is scheduled for June 10, 2024, to finalize the details.

Council Member Chen asked about the budget allocation for the summer events.
City Manager Sterling responded that the budget for the summer events is $150,000, which is funded by the City's Special Events Fund.
Council Member O'Malley asked about the contingency plan for inclement weather.
City Manager Sterling stated that the contingency plan includes the availability of indoor venues and the postponement of events in case of severe weather.
Council Member Thompson asked about the vendor selection process.
City Manager Sterling responded that vendors are selected through a competitive bidding process, with priority given to local businesses.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member Miller, to approve the Inter-Departmental Memo regarding the coordination of summer events and to schedule the joint planning meeting for June 10, 2024.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

ITEM 16: PERMIT APPLICATIONS – ARCHITECTURAL SUMMARIES

Planning Director Elena R. Rossi presented the Permit Applications with Architectural Summaries for the following projects:

1. Project ID 2024-001: Residential Addition at 789 Maple Drive.
   Applicant: Michael J. O'Brien.
   Description: A two-story addition to the rear of the existing single-family home. The addition will add 800 square feet of living space, including a master bedroom and a bathroom. The architectural summary indicates that the design is consistent with the existing style of the home. The proposed setbacks are in compliance with the R-2 zoning district.
   Status: Pending Review.

2. Project ID 2024-002: Commercial Renovation at 123 Main Street.
   Applicant: Millbrook Retail Group, LLC.
   Description: The renovation of the ground floor of the building to accommodate a new retail space. The architectural summary indicates that the renovation will include the installation of new storefront windows and a new entrance. The proposed changes are in compliance with the Downtown Commercial Zoning District.
   Status: Pending Review.

3. Project ID 2024-003: Mixed-Use Development at 456 River Road.
   Applicant: Riverfront Development, Inc.
   Description: The development of a mixed-use building with residential units on the upper floors and commercial space on the ground floor. The architectural summary indicates that the building will be 10 stories tall. The proposed setbacks are subject to a variance.
   Status: Pending Review.

Council Member Miller asked about the timeline for the review of these applications.
Director Rossi responded that the review period is expected to be 30 days for each application.
Council Member O'Malley asked if the applications have been notified to the neighbors.
Director Rossi confirmed that the applications have been notified to the neighbors within the required radius.
Council Member Thompson asked about the funding sources for the projects.
Director Rossi stated that the funding sources are private, and no City funds are being used.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member O'Malley, to accept the Permit Applications with Architectural Summaries and to direct the Planning Department to proceed with the review process.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

ITEM 17: CAMPAIGN FINANCE FILINGS – DETAILED REVIEW

City Clerk Thomas J. Halloway presented the detailed review of the Campaign Finance Filings for the current quarter. The review included the following filings:

1. Filing ID CF-2024-Q2: Quarterly Contribution Report - Q2.
   Author: Millbrook Ethics Commission.
   Recipients: Public.
   Status: Complete.
   Details: The report includes contributions from Henderson Construction ($1,000), Local Bank & Trust ($2,500), and Green Valley Farms ($500). The total contributions received were $12,500. ITEM 18: BUDGET ADJUSTMENT – CAPITAL IMPROVEMENTS

City Manager Richard D. Sterling presented the Budget Adjustment for Capital Improvements. The adjustment is required to accommodate the additional costs associated with the Downtown Street Resurfacing Project and the Millbrook Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade Project. The total adjustment amount is $125,000. The funds will be transferred from the General Fund Reserve to the Capital Improvements Fund.

The adjustment includes the following line items:

- Millbrook Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade Project: $75,000.
- Miscellaneous Capital Improvements: $0.

ITEM 2: VARIANCE REQUEST FOR 450 RIVER ROAD

Zoning Board Administrator Marcus T. Webb introduced the variance request for 450 River Road. The applicant, Mr. James P. Henderson, is seeking a variance to allow the construction of a two-story accessory dwelling unit (ADU) and a detached garage structure that exceeds the current setback requirements by 4.5 feet. The proposed structure is to be used for short-term rental purposes.

The applicant presented the case, highlighting the property's location in a high-demand area for short-term rentals and the architectural design of the proposed structure. The applicant submitted a traffic impact study, an architectural rendering, and a letter of support from the immediate neighbors.

The Planning Director presented the staff report, recommending the denial of the variance request. The staff report noted that the proposed setback variance of 4.5 feet does not meet the criteria for a variance under Section 14.05 of the Millbrook Zoning Ordinance. The staff argued that the property is not unique in its physical characteristics and that the hardship is self-imposed. Furthermore, the staff noted that the proposed use as a short-term rental may create a nuisance for the surrounding residential area.

Public Comment:
Mr. James P. Henderson, the applicant, spoke in support of the variance. He stated that the variance is necessary to maximize the utility of the property and that the structure will not negatively impact the character of the neighborhood. He emphasized the economic benefits of the short-term rental and the architectural design of the proposed structure.
Ms. John D. Smith, a resident of 450 River Road, spoke against the variance. She expressed concern about the potential increase in traffic and noise. She requested that the Board deny the variance.
Ms. Sarah L. Johnson, the Vice Chairperson, asked the applicant if he had addressed the noise concerns raised in the traffic impact study.
Mr. Henderson responded that he had proposed a sound barrier wall along the property line, which is included in the architectural rendering.
Member Davis asked if the neighbors had signed the letter of support.
Mr. Henderson confirmed that the letter of support was signed by the owners of 448 and 452 River Road.

Board Discussion:
Chairperson Miller asked the Board members to consider the criteria for a variance under Section 14.05 of the Millbrook Zoning Ordinance.
Vice Chairperson Johnson noted that the property is not unique in its physical characteristics.
Member Smith noted that the hardship is self-imposed.
Member Brown noted that the proposed use as a short-term rental may create a nuisance for the surrounding residential area.
Member Davis noted that the applicant has proposed a sound barrier wall, but the variance is still not justified.

Motion by Member Smith, seconded by Member Brown, to deny the variance request for 450 River Road.

Roll Call Vote:
Chairperson Miller: Aye
Vice Chairperson Johnson: Aye
Member Smith: Aye
Member Brown: Aye
Member Davis: Aye

Motion Carried.

INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMO
DATE: JUNE 4, 2024
TO: ALL DEPARTMENT HEADS
FROM: Richard D. Sterling, City Manager
SUBJECT: COORDINATION OF SUMMER EVENTS

This memo outlines the responsibilities of various departments for the upcoming summer festival, the farmers market, and the outdoor concert series.

DEPARTMENTAL RESPONSIBILITIES:
1. Public Works Department: Responsible for setting up stages, lighting, and sound equipment.
2. Police Department: Responsible for security, traffic control, and emergency response.
3. Parks and Recreation Department: Responsible for vendor coordination, food service, and activity planning.
4. Planning Department: Responsible for permit issuance and zoning compliance.
5. Finance Department: Responsible for budget oversight and revenue collection.
6. Communications Department: Responsible for public relations and media outreach.

The memo emphasizes the need for close coordination between departments to ensure the success of the events. A joint planning meeting is scheduled for June 10, 2024, to finalize the details.

BUDGET ALLOCATION: $150,000 (Funded by the City's Special Events Fund).
CONTINGENCY PLAN: Availability of indoor venues and postponement of events in case of severe weather.
VENDOR SELECTION PROCESS: Competitive bidding process, with priority given to local businesses.

NOTES:
1. The joint planning meeting is scheduled for June 10, 2024.
2. The budget is $150,000.
3. The contingency plan includes the availability of indoor venues and the postponement of events in case of severe weather.
4. The vendor selection process is a competitive bidding process, with priority given to local businesses.

### DOCUMENT 1: OFFICIAL MINUTES
**MEETING OF THE OAKHAVEN PARKS AND RECREATION COMMISSION**
**DATE:** October 10, 2023
**TIME:** 6:00 P.M.
**LOCATION:** City Hall, Council Chambers, 100 Civic Center Drive, Oakhaven
**PRESIDING OFFICER:** Commissioner Elena Rodriguez, Chair
**ATTENDANCE:**
Commissioners: Elena Rodriguez (Chair), Marcus Thorne, Sarah Jenkins, David Chen, Robert "Bob" Miller.
City Staff Present: Director of Parks and Recreation, Thomas H. Vance; Finance Director, Linda K. O'Malley; Assistant City Attorney, Michael S. Sterling.
Public Present: 14 individuals.

**CALL TO ORDER**
The meeting was called to order at 6:00 P.M. by Chair Rodriguez. A quorum was established.

**ROLL CALL**
Commissioner Rodriguez: "I will take the roll call. Commissioner Thorne?"
Commissioner Thorne: "Present."
Commissioner Jenkins: "Present."
Commissioner Chen: "Present."
Commissioner Miller: "Present."
Commissioner Rodriguez: "I am present. A quorum is established. We will proceed with the agenda."

**APPROVAL OF AGENDA**
Motion by Commissioner Thorne, seconded by Commissioner Jenkins, to approve the agenda as distributed.
Vote on Motion 23-10-A:
Commissioner Rodriguez: Aye.
Commissioner Thorne: Aye.
Commissioner Jenkins: Aye.
Commissioner Chen: Aye.
Commissioner Miller: Nay.
Motion Carried 4-1.

**CONSENT CALENDAR**
Items listed on the consent calendar were considered a routine matter and voted upon as a group unless a member requested a separate discussion.
Motion by Commissioner Chen, seconded by Commissioner Thorne, to approve the consent calendar.
Items included:
1. Approval of September 12, 2023, meeting minutes.
2. Approval of the September 2023 utility payment schedule for city parks.
3. Approval of the seasonal maintenance contract for the Oakhaven Golf Course, extension of term through December 31, 2023.
Vote on Motion 23-10-B:
Commissioner Rodriguez: Aye.
Commissioner Thorne: Aye.
Commissioner Jenkins: Aye.
Commissioner Chen: Aye.
Commissioner Miller: Aye.
Motion Carried 5-0.

**STAFF REPORT: WINTER OPERATIONS AND INFRASTRUCTURE READINESS**
Director Thomas H. Vance took the podium to present the staff report regarding winter operations planning for the upcoming season. The report covered snow removal logistics, ice resurfacing requirements for the outdoor rink, and equipment maintenance schedules.

Director Vance stated, "The primary objective for the winter season is the preservation of safety and accessibility for our three major recreational facilities: the Central Ice Arena, the Northridge Community Park, and the Riverside Trail System. Our current inventory of snow removal equipment is aging. The plow trucks assigned to the Northridge sector have accumulated over 12,000 miles since their last major overhaul. Furthermore, the ice resurfacing machine, Unit #442, has a documented history of hydraulic fluid leaks that have required frequent patching."

The Director presented a cost-benefit analysis for the proposed equipment lease. "We are proposing a lease agreement with 'Arctic Solutions LLC' for two heavy-duty snow plows and one ice resurfacing unit for a period of twelve months, commencing November 1, 2023. The total cost of the lease is $145,000. This arrangement includes full maintenance coverage and a 24-hour response time for mechanical failures, which is critical during the peak winter months when road and facility access are compromised by weather events."

Director Vance continued, "Staff recommends this lease over a purchase because the capital expenditure required for purchasing new units exceeds the current fiscal year's capital reserve allocation. The lease option allows us to meet our operational mandates without depleting funds designated for the upcoming park renovation projects. The lease terms are non-cancelable except in the event of force majeure. The vendor, Arctic Solutions LLC, has a rating of A- from the municipal bond rating agency."

**PUBLIC COMMENT: WINTER OPERATIONS**
The floor was opened to public comment.

**Comment 1:** James P. O'Brien, resident of 402 Elm Street.
Mr. O'Brien stated, "I am concerned about the timing of the equipment lease. The city has a history of delaying snow removal until after the first major storm. If we lease this equipment, I want to know if the drivers will be trained on the new systems. The last time we had a new plow, the operator crashed it into a light pole because he wasn't familiar with the hydraulics. I request that the commission require a training certificate before the equipment is deployed."

Director Vance responded, "The lease agreement with Arctic Solutions LLC includes a mandatory three-day training program for all city operators. This is a contractual obligation. We will not deploy the new units until the training is complete and verified by the vendor."

**Comment 2:** Maria Gonzalez, resident of 889 Oakhaven Drive.
Ms. Gonzalez stated, "The ice resurfacing machine is the heart of our winter season. If that machine fails, the rink is closed. I am worried that a lease might mean we are renting a machine that is already at the end of its lifecycle. I suggest we look at a purchase option at the end of the year instead."

Commissioner Chen asked, "Does the lease include a purchase option at the end of the term?"
Director Vance replied, "Yes, Commissioner. The lease includes a fair market value purchase option at the end of the twelve-month term. This gives the city flexibility to buy the equipment if it proves reliable, or return it if it does not meet our needs."

**Comment 3:** Robert Miller, resident of 112 Pine Lane.
Mr. Miller stated, "I have been a park user for twenty years. I believe the city is spending too much on equipment and not enough on the actual ice quality. The ice has been thin the last two years. I think we should divert some of this lease money to a new ice plant rather than just renting a plow."

Commissioner Rodriguez interjected, "Mr. Miller, please address the motion at hand. We are discussing the lease for snow removal and ice resurfacing equipment, not the ice plant."

**STAFF RECOMMENDATION**
Director Vance concluded the presentation by stating, "Staff recommends the approval of the lease agreement with Arctic Solutions LLC for the sum of $145,000. This is necessary to ensure the continuity of winter operations and the safety of park users. The Finance Director has verified that the funds are available within the Parks and Recreation Operating Budget under Line Item 405-22. The contract will be reviewed by the City Attorney prior to execution."

**MOTION TO APPROVE EQUIPMENT LEASE**
Commissioner Thorne moved to approve the lease agreement with Arctic Solutions LLC for the sum of $145,000, as recommended by staff, with the condition that the training program be completed prior to deployment.
Commissioner Jenkins seconded the motion.

**DEBATE**
Commissioner Miller: "I must reiterate my concern. We are leasing equipment that we could potentially buy. The long-term cost of leasing is higher than buying. However, I understand the cash flow constraints. I will vote nay on this motion because I believe we should pursue a different financing strategy, perhaps a capital bond measure."

Commissioner Chen: "I support the motion. The lease provides immediate access to reliable equipment. The training requirement addresses the safety concerns raised by Mr. O'Brien. The purchase option provides future flexibility."

Commissioner Jenkins: "I support the motion. The staff analysis clearly shows that purchasing is not feasible within the current budget. We must ensure the rink is open."

Commissioner Thorne: "I support the motion. The conditions regarding training are acceptable. We are moving forward with the lease."

**VOTE ON MOTION 23-10-C**
The Chair called for a vote on the motion to approve the lease agreement with Arctic Solutions LLC.

**Roll Call Vote:**
Commissioner Rodriguez: Aye.
Commissioner Thorne: Aye.
Commissioner Jenkins: Aye.
Commissioner Chen: Aye.
Commissioner Miller: Nay.

**Result:** Motion Carried 4-1.

**ADJOURNMENT**
No further business was presented. Motion by Commissioner Chen, seconded by Commissioner Jenkins, to adjourn the meeting.
Vote on Motion 23-10-D: All members present voted Aye.
The meeting was adjourned at 7:15 P.M.

**CERTIFICATION**

### DOCUMENT 2: PERMIT APPLICATION AND ARCHITECTURAL SUMMARY
**CITY OF OAKHAVEN**
**BUILDING AND SAFETY DEPARTMENT**
**PERMIT APPLICATION SUBMISSION**

**APPLICATION NUMBER:** 2023-BLD-11-0058
**DATE SUBMITTED:** November 5, 2023
**DATE OF RECEIPT:** November 5, 2023
**STATUS:** Under Review / Pending Final Approval

**PROJECT DETAILS**
**Project Name:** Central Ice Arena Mechanical System Upgrade
**Applicant:** City of Oakhaven, Parks and Recreation Department
**Property Owner:** City of Oakhaven
**Property Address:** 500 Civic Center Drive, Oakhaven, CA 94000
**Parcel ID:** 9900-Alpha
**Project Type:** Public Facility Renovation / Mechanical System Upgrade
**Estimated Completion Date:** February 28, 2024
**Total Estimated Cost:** $180,000.00
**Funding Source:** Parks and Recreation Operating Budget, Line Item 405-22

**SCOPE OF WORK**
The applicant requests a building permit to renovate the mechanical refrigeration and ventilation systems of the Central Ice Arena. The existing system, installed in 1998, has reached the end of its operational life cycle. The proposed work includes the removal of the current ammonia-based refrigeration units, the installation of a new direct-expansion glycol system, and the upgrade of the ventilation ductwork to meet current energy efficiency standards.

**TECHNICAL SPECIFICATIONS**
1.  **Refrigeration System:** Installation of a new 150-ton Direct Expansion (DX) Glycol Chiller Unit. The new unit will utilize R-448A refrigerant, which is compliant with the latest Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) regulations regarding ozone depletion. The system will be connected to the existing concrete foundation with new vibration isolation pads.
2.  **Piping and Valving:** Replacement of all copper and steel piping within the arena floor slab. New piping will consist of high-density polyethylene (HDPE) insulated tubing with a 2-inch diameter. All valves will be ball valves with remote actuation capabilities for emergency shut-off.
3.  **Ventilation:** Upgrade of the HVAC system to include variable air volume (VAV) boxes. The new system will maintain a relative humidity level between 40% and 60% and a temperature of 55°F (12.8°C) within the arena.
4.  **Electrical:** Installation of a new 480V, 3-phase electrical panel dedicated to the mechanical system. This includes the replacement of all control wiring and the integration of a Building Automation System (BAS) for remote monitoring.
5.  **Ice Rink Surface:** Minor resurfacing of the concrete slab to accommodate the new piping layout. The existing urethane coating will be stripped and replaced with a new high-gloss urethane finish.

**ARCHITECTURAL SUMMARY**
**Prepared by:** Oakhaven Architectural Group, LLC
**Architect of Record:** Jonathan P. Miller, AIA, License No. CA-23456
**Date of Architectural Summary:** November 1, 2023

**1.0 Existing Conditions**
The Central Ice Arena is a single-story structure with a steel truss roof and concrete block walls. The building footprint is 120 feet by 60 feet. The current mechanical room is located in the northeast corner of the building. The existing refrigeration system is housed in a separate exterior enclosure that has shown signs of corrosion. The interior of the arena features a 20,000 square foot ice surface surrounded by a 10-foot wide spectator walkway. The existing ventilation system is insufficient to handle the humidity load generated by the ice, leading to condensation on the ceiling and walls.

**2.0 Proposed Modifications**
The proposed modifications involve the interior of the mechanical room and the sub-slab area. No changes are proposed to the exterior façade, roof structure, or building footprint. The new mechanical equipment will be installed within the existing mechanical room footprint. The new refrigeration unit will be placed on a raised concrete pad to facilitate maintenance access. The ventilation ductwork will be rerouted through the existing ceiling plenum.

**2.1 Mechanical Room Layout**
The new refrigeration unit will occupy 15% of the existing mechanical room floor space. The remaining space will be utilized for the new electrical panel and the BAS control cabinet. The layout ensures a minimum clearance of 3 feet around all equipment for maintenance access, in compliance with the International Mechanical Code (IMC) Section 304.

**2.2 Sub-Slab Piping**
The new HDPE piping will be installed in a serpentine pattern beneath the concrete slab. The depth of the piping will be 12 inches below the top of the slab. The piping will be encased in a 2-inch layer of sand to prevent abrasion. The connection points to the ice surface will be sealed with a waterproof epoxy compound.

**2.3 Ceiling and Ventilation**
The existing ceiling tiles will be removed to allow for the installation of new ventilation ducts. The new ducts will be insulated with 2-inch fiberglass insulation to prevent condensation. The ducts will be suspended from the existing steel trusses using vibration isolators. The air supply will be directed towards the spectator areas to minimize drafts on the ice surface.

**3.0 Compliance and Standards**
The proposed design complies with the following codes and standards:
*   California Building Code (CBC) 2022 Edition
*   International Mechanical Code (IMC) 2021 Edition
*   National Electrical Code (NEC) 2020 Edition
*   City of Oakhaven Zoning Ordinance
*   American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) Standard 90.1-2019

**4.0 Structural Considerations**
A structural engineer's report has been prepared by Structural Dynamics, Inc. The report confirms that the existing foundation and roof structure can support the additional weight of the new mechanical equipment. The new equipment will be distributed evenly across the mechanical room floor to prevent point loading. The roof trusses will not be modified.

**5.0 Environmental Considerations**
The new refrigeration system utilizes R-448A, a low-global warming potential (GWP) refrigerant. The system is designed to operate at 20% higher efficiency than the current system, resulting in a projected energy savings of 15,000 kWh per year. The project will not generate any hazardous waste, as the old refrigerant will be recovered and disposed of by a certified recycling firm.

**6.0 Construction Timeline**
The construction is scheduled to begin on December 15, 2023, and is expected to be completed by February 28, 2024. The work will be performed during off-hours (10:00 P.M. to 6:00 A.M.) to minimize disruption to public use of the facility. The ice resurfacing machine will be leased during the construction period to ensure the rink can be prepared for the final ice pour upon completion.

**7.0 Safety Measures**
The contractor will implement the following safety measures:
*   Barricading of the work area to prevent public access.
*   Use of personal protective equipment (PPE) including hard hats, safety glasses, and gloves.
*   Continuous air monitoring for refrigerant leaks.
*   Fire suppression system activation during welding operations.

**8.0 Cost Estimate**
The total estimated cost of the project is $180,000. This includes:
*   Equipment and Materials: $110,000
*   Labor: $55,000
*   Permits and Fees: $5,000
*   Contingency (10%): $10,000

**9.0 Certifications**
I, Jonathan P. Miller, hereby certify that the plans and specifications submitted herein are accurate and comply with all applicable codes and regulations.

__________________________
Jonathan P. Miller, AIA
Oakhaven Architectural Group, LLC
Date: November 1, 2023

**Notes:** The electrical panel location requires verification against the existing fire alarm system. A revised electrical drawing must be submitted prior to final permit issuance. The contractor must provide proof of liability insurance and workers' compensation coverage.

### DOCUMENT 4: INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMO
**CITY OF OAKHAVEN**
**OFFICE OF THE CITY MANAGER**
**INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMORANDUM**

**TO:**
Director Thomas H. Vance, Parks and Recreation
Chief of Police, Officer James R. Miller
Director of Public Works, Susan L. Wright
**FROM:**
Deputy City Manager, Robert J. Thompson
**DATE:** December 1, 2023
**SUBJECT:** Safety Protocol Update - Park Access Points
**REFERENCE:** City Council Resolution 2023-15
**ATTACHMENTS:**
1.  Site Plan for North Entrance Signage
2.  Lighting Inspection Checklist

**1.0 PURPOSE**
The purpose of this memorandum is to establish updated safety protocols for the North Entrance of the Central Park and to coordinate the installation of new signage and lighting improvements. This initiative is in response to a recent increase in after-hours incidents at the park and the City Council's directive to enhance public safety in recreational areas.

**2.0 SIGNAGE INSTALLATION**
The Parks and Recreation Department is responsible for the design and installation of new signage at the North Entrance. The signage will include:
*   Park Hours of Operation (6:00 A.M. to 10:00 P.M.)
*   Prohibited Activities (Alcohol, Pets, Fires)
*   Emergency Contact Information (911 and Park Ranger Dispatch)
*   "No Trespassing" Warning

The signage will be manufactured from high-density polyethylene (HDPE) with reflective lettering to ensure visibility during low-light conditions. The signs will be mounted on galvanized steel posts, 8 feet in height, and will be installed at a distance of 50 feet from the entrance gate.

**3.0 LIGHTING INSPECTION AND COORDINATION**
The Public Works Department, in coordination with the Police Department, is responsible for the inspection of the existing lighting fixtures at the North Entrance. The current lighting system consists of four 150-watt metal halide fixtures, which are approaching the end of their useful life.

**3.1 Inspection Protocol**
The Police Department will conduct a joint inspection with Public Works on December 5, 2023. The inspection will focus on:
*   Illumination levels at the entrance and surrounding pathways.
*   Functionality of all lighting fixtures.
*   Presence of shadows or dark spots that could conceal unauthorized activity.
*   Condition of the electrical wiring and control boxes.

**3.2 Upgrade Requirements**
If the inspection reveals deficiencies, the Public Works Department will replace the existing fixtures with LED luminaires. The new fixtures will provide 2,000 lumens of light and will be equipped with motion sensors to reduce energy consumption during off-peak hours. The installation will be completed by December 15, 2023.

**4.0 POLICE DEPARTMENT COORDINATION**
The Police Department is requested to:
*   Increase patrol frequency at the North Entrance between the hours of 9:00 P.M. and 10:00 P.M.
*   Review the park's security camera footage for the past three months to identify any patterns of activity.
*   Provide a written report on the effectiveness of the new lighting and signage by January 15, 2024.

**5.0 TIMELINE**
*   **December 5, 2023:** Joint lighting inspection.
*   **December 8, 2023:** Finalize signage design and order materials.
*   **December 12, 2023:** Install new signage.
*   **December 15, 2023:** Complete lighting upgrades (if necessary).
*   **January 15, 2024:** Submit final report to the City Manager.

**6.0 BUDGET IMPLICATIONS**
The cost of the signage installation is estimated at $2,500. The cost of the lighting upgrade is estimated at $4,000. These costs will be covered by the Public Safety Fund, Line Item 305-10.

**7.0 CONTACT INFORMATION**
For questions regarding this memorandum, please contact:
*   Parks and Recreation: Thomas H. Vance, (555) 123-4567
*   Police Department: Officer James R. Miller, (555) 987-6543
*   Public Works: Susan L. Wright, (555) 456-7890

**APPROVAL**
This memorandum has been reviewed and approved by the Deputy City Manager.

__________________________
Robert J. Thompson
Deputy City Manager
Date: December 1, 2023

### DOCUMENT 6: ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW SUMMARY
**CITY OF OAKHAVEN**
**ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW OFFICE**
**ENVIRONMENTAL ASSESSMENT SUMMARY**

**PROJECT:** Central Ice Arena Mechanical System Upgrade
**PROJECT ID:** 2023-ENV-11-005
**DATE OF PREPARATION:** November 10, 2023
**PREPARED BY:** Environmental Review Office, City of Oakhaven

**1.0 PROJECT DESCRIPTION**
The proposed project involves the renovation of the mechanical refrigeration and ventilation systems of the Central Ice Arena. The project includes the removal of the existing ammonia-based refrigeration units and the installation of a new direct-expansion glycol system. The project also includes the upgrade of the ventilation ductwork and the installation of a new electrical panel.

**2.0 ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS**
**2.1 Air Quality**
The new refrigeration system will utilize R-448A refrigerant, which has a lower global warming potential (GWP) than the current refrigerant. The project will result in a reduction of greenhouse gas emissions during the operational phase. The construction phase will involve the use of diesel-powered equipment, which may result in temporary increases in particulate matter and nitrogen oxides. However, these emissions will be minimal and short-term.

**2.2 Water Quality**
The project does not involve any discharge of pollutants into surface or groundwater. The installation of the new piping will require the excavation of the concrete slab, which may disturb soil. The contractor will implement erosion control measures to prevent sediment runoff. The old refrigerant will be recovered and disposed of by a certified recycling firm, ensuring that no harmful substances are released into the environment.

**2.3 Noise and Vibration**
The installation of the new refrigeration unit will generate noise during the construction phase. The contractor will be required to limit work hours to 10:00 A.M. to 6:00 P.M. to minimize noise disturbance to the surrounding neighborhood. The new unit will be equipped with vibration isolation pads to reduce noise during operation.

**2.4 Waste Generation**
The project will generate construction waste, including old piping, electrical wiring, and insulation. The contractor will be required to recycle all construction waste materials. The old refrigerant will be recovered and recycled.

**2.5 Energy Consumption**
The new refrigeration system is designed to operate at 20% higher efficiency than the current system. This will result in a projected energy savings of 15,000 kWh per year. The new ventilation system will also be more energy-efficient, further reducing the overall energy consumption of the arena.

**3.0 MITIGATION MEASURES**
**3.1 Construction Phase**
*   The contractor will implement erosion control measures to prevent sediment runoff.
*   Work hours will be limited to 10:00 A.M. to 6:00 P.M. to minimize noise disturbance.
*   All construction waste will be recycled.
*   The old refrigerant will be recovered and recycled by a certified firm.

**3.2 Operational Phase**
*   The new refrigeration system will be monitored to ensure that it operates within the specified parameters.
*   The ventilation system will be maintained to ensure that it operates efficiently.

**4.0 CONCLUSION**
Based on the environmental review, the proposed project will not have a significant adverse impact on the environment. The project complies with all applicable environmental regulations and standards. The mitigation measures identified in this assessment will ensure that any potential impacts are minimized.

**5.0 RECOMMENDATION**
It is recommended that the City Council approve the environmental assessment and issue a Notice of Determination.

**CERTIFICATION**
I, the undersigned, do hereby certify that the foregoing environmental assessment is a true and correct summary of the environmental impacts of the proposed project.

__________________________
Dr. Emily R. Green
Environmental Review Officer
City of Oakhaven
Date: November 10, 2023

This memorandum serves to document the findings of the Legal Department regarding the ownership structure of the applicant entity, Crestview Holdings LLC. The purpose of this review was to verify beneficial ownership and ensure compliance with municipal disclosure requirements for zoning variance requests.

The Legal Department conducted a comprehensive search of the State Business Registry and local corporate filing databases. The investigation focused on the relationship between Crestview Holdings LLC and associated entities listed in the application materials. The search confirmed the existence of multiple limited liability companies associated with the primary applicant.

The Legal Department completed the review of the corporate filings. A legal opinion was drafted based on the data retrieved from the public records. 1.  Crestview Holdings LLC is the primary applicant for the zoning variance.
2.  Three additional entities share a registered agent address with the primary applicant.

4.  The common address is utilized for service of process and official state notifications.

"Marcus Webb controls Crestview Holdings and three intermediary LLCs that share the same registered agent."

"Ownership structure obscures true control."

"Legal review confirms common agent."

III. OWNERSHIP VERIFICATION FINDINGS

This memorandum serves to document the findings of the Legal Department regarding the ownership structure of the applicant entity, Crestview Holdings LLC. The purpose of this review was to verify beneficial ownership and ensure compliance with municipal disclosure requirements for zoning variance requests.

The Legal Department conducted a comprehensive search of the State Business Registry and local corporate filing databases. The investigation focused on the relationship between Crestview Holdings LLC and associated entities listed in the application materials. The search confirmed the existence of multiple limited liability companies associated with the primary applicant.

The Legal Department completed the review of the corporate filings. A legal opinion was drafted based on the data retrieved from the public records. Ownership was verified through the cross-referencing of registered agent information and principal officer listings. A recommendation was made regarding the transparency of the ownership structure. The document was filed in the Legal Files repository under the designated tracking ID.

The review of the corporate filings indicates the following:

1.  Crestview Holdings LLC is the primary applicant for the zoning variance.
2.  Three additional entities share a registered agent address with the primary applicant.
3.  The registered agent address is a commercial property located at a specific industrial zone.
4.  The common address is utilized for service of process and official state notifications.

The Legal Department observes that the registered agent information for the primary applicant and the associated entities matches exactly. The address listed for the registered agent is consistent across all filings.

The Legal Department notes the following regarding the corporate structure:
"Marcus Webb controls Crestview Holdings and three intermediary LLCs that share the same registered agent."

Further analysis of the corporate filings indicates a consolidation of control through the shared service address. The Legal Department observes:
"Ownership structure obscures true control."

A formal confirmation was generated by the Office of the Secretary of State. The Legal Department confirms:
"Legal review confirms common agent."

Based on the findings of this investigation, the Legal Department determines that the current disclosure is insufficient for public transparency. The Legal Department states:
"Disclosure required for public record."

The denial was not arbitrary but was grounded in specific technical deficiencies identified during the review process:

*   **Technicality Cited:** The Order of Denial (ORD-2024-28) formally stated that the application was denied based on the "technicality cited: Setback Calculation" [zoning_corruption_11_ep_010].

*   **Environmental Compliance:** The project had already passed its environmental review, with no significant adverse impacts identified regarding air quality, water resources, or wildlife [zoning_corruption_11_ep_009]. This suggests the denial was not due to environmental concerns.

**Conclusion**
The denial of the Millbrook Community Land Trust project was based on **legitimate reasons**.
